old_claim.note: Printed Financial Price Lists provides a capability that enables this technology without being the only possible path.

new_claim.note: Auction houses depended on lot descriptions, appraisals, sale catalogues, and sale-result records rather than on printed financial price lists.

source_shape.source_locator: Investopedia's operations section says auctioneers appraise lot items, form an idea of worth, and open bidding with a suggested bid. Harvard Library says its auction-sales catalogue collection includes more than 40,000 catalogues from the 18th century to present, including rare and annotated catalogues used for provenance research and sale information.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: The sources document lot appraisal, catalogue records, and sale information as auction-house information infrastructure.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: Auction-house records concern lots, valuations, provenance, and sale results; printed financial price lists are market quotation artifacts and are too narrow for this node.

ontology_before: Commercial Auction Houses depended on printed financial price lists, implying market-quotation publication enabled auction-house operations.
ontology_after: Commercial Auction Houses depend on record keeping because lot descriptions, appraisals, catalogues, and sale-result records are the relevant information substrate.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Changed: commercial_auction_houses now depends on record_keeping and no longer directly depends on printed_financial_price_lists.
would_reject_if: The node is narrowed to printed auction-price current lists rather than auction-house operations.
A stronger source shows Stockholm's 1674 auction-house model directly depended on printed financial-price-list infrastructure.
A dedicated auction_sales_catalogs node is added and replaces record_keeping as the precise record substrate.
short_reason: Auction houses need lot and sale records, not generic financial price lists.
